coredata.dc:title: Enhancing the mechanical and electrical conductivity of copper matrix composites through grafting carbonized polymer dots (CPD) onto carbon nanotube surfaces
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wenfu
Liu, Liang
Bao, Rui
Yi, Jianhong
Yang, Yajie
Li, Caiju
Chen, Xiaofeng
Guo, Shengda
coredata.dc:description: Tailoring the structural design of reinforcement is an effective strategy to attain the homogeneous dispersion and robust interface bonding in carbon nanotubes reinforced copper (CNTs/Cu) composites. Herein, a novel zero-dimensional carbonized polymer dots (CPD) was synthesized and grafted onto the CNTs surfaces via a typical one-step hydrothermal method. The resultant reinforcement, CPD@CNTs, exhibited good dispersibility due to the abundant polar functional groups on CPD. Furthermore, the interface adhesion was also ameliorated by means of the in-situ formed Cu2O transient phase. The CPD@CNTs/Cu composite shows outstanding mechanical performances, the yield strength and tensile strength had increased to 269 MPa and 308 MPa, while maintained excellent ductility (43 %). Moreover, the electrical conductivity of the composite remains at a high level of 95.3 %IACS. Our findings suggest that adjusting the structural configuration of reinforcement is a feasible approach to optimize dispersion and interfacial structure.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper matrix composites
Carbon nanotubes (CNTs)
Carbonized polymer dots (CPD)
Mechanical performances
Electrical conductivity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the hybrid CPD@CNTs powders

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Major Science and Technology Projects in Yunnan Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fabrication process of CPD@CNTs/Cu composite.

TEM and HRTEM images of (a-b) CPD; (c-d) pristine CNTs and (e-f) CPD@CNTs powders, the insets in (a) is corresponding photos of CPD in daylight (left) and ultraviolet light (right) and insets in (c) and (e) are corresponding pictures of pristine CNTs and CPD@CNTs powders dispersed in ethanol by 0.5 h sonication then kept quietly for 24 h, respectively; (I-II) FFT and IFFT images of the white frame in (f); (g) the histogram of particle size statistic of CPD on surfaces of CNTs.
XRD patterns of (a) CPD, CNTs and CPD@CNTs powders; XPS survey spectra of (b) CPD; the C1s and O1s high-resolution XPS spectra of (c-d) CPD.

Optical micrographs of sintered samples: (a) pure Cu, (b) CPD/Cu, (c) CNTs/Cu, (d) CPD@CNTs/Cu; (e-h) the corresponding grain size distribution histograms of (a-d) respectively; SEM images of (i) CNTs/Cu and (j) CPD@CNTs/Cu composite.

(a-b) TEM images showing the morphology of CPD@CNTs/Cu composite, the inset in (b) is EDS analysis of the selected region of CPD@CNTs; (c) HRTEM image disclosing the interface between CPD and CNTs; (d) TEM images revealing the interface between CPD@CNTs and Cu matrix; (e) magnified image of the white frame in (d), the insets in (e) are corresponding FFT and IFFT images of the white frame in (e); (f) interface microstructure of CPD@CNTs and Cu2O.

(a) Histogram of relative density and electrical conductivity and hardness; (b) engineering tensile stress-strain curves; (c) comparisons of tensile properties with previously reported Cu composites [11,14,17,20,28–33].
SEM images of the fracture surface of (a) pure Cu, (b) CPD/Cu, (c) CNTs/Cu, (d) CPD@CNTs/Cu composites.
A comparison between the experimental value and estimated yield strength of CPD@CNTs/Cu composite.
Schematic diagrams of CPD@CNTs distribution and corresponding fracture mechanisms.
Tensile mechanical properties of pure Cu and composites.

head.ce:title: Enhancing the mechanical and electrical conductivity of copper matrix composites through grafting carbonized polymer dots (CPD) onto carbon nanotube surfaces

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, 650093 China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials of Yunnan Province, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials of Yunnan Province
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials of Yunnan Province, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650093 China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou, Jiangxi 341000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry
Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou, Jiangxi, 341000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tailoring the structural design of reinforcement is an effective strategy to attain the homogeneous dispersion and robust interface bonding in carbon nanotubes reinforced copper (CNTs/Cu) composites. Herein, a novel zero-dimensional carbonized polymer dots (CPD) was synthesized and grafted onto the CNTs surfaces via a typical one-step hydrothermal method. The resultant reinforcement, CPD@CNTs, exhibited good dispersibility due to the abundant polar functional groups on CPD. Furthermore, the interface adhesion was also ameliorated by means of the in-situ formed Cu2O transient phase. The CPD@CNTs/Cu composite shows outstanding mechanical performances, the yield strength and tensile strength had increased to 269 MPa and 308 MPa, while maintained excellent ductility (43 %). Moreover, the electrical conductivity of the composite remains at a high level of 95.3 %IACS. Our findings suggest that adjusting the structural configuration of reinforcement is a feasible approach to optimize dispersion and interfacial structure.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The advanced carbon nanomaterials with extraordinary mechanical and physicochemical properties, represented by carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene nanosheets, are regarded as ideal reinforcements for developing high performance composites [1,2]. Due to the urgent need in transportation, machinery manufacturing and various electronic products fields, during the past two decades, efforts have been made to develop carbon nanotubes reinforced copper matrix composites (CNTs/Cu) [3–5]. However, this kind of composite has not been come into practice yet, owing to the lack of efficient mass production route that can realize high strength combined with good electrical conductivity [6–8].

In order to obtain CNTs/Cu composites with satisfactory comprehensive properties, attaining homogeneous dispersion of CNTs is an important precondition [9,10]. Currently, functionalized CNTs have been utilized to solve the dispersion issue. For instance, Zhang et al. [10] used a mixture of concentrated nitric acid and sulphuric acid to oxidize CNTs and then mixed with Cu powders by wet mixing, the ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of CNTs/Cu bulk composites increased by 46 % as compared with pure Cu. Although the functionalization of CNTs can induce a variety of oxygen-containing functional groups, which promotes CNTs dispersion, it causes structure damage of the CNTs and this strategy is low cost-effective [11]. Besides, it is well accepted that the robust interface bonding is advantageous for load transfer and electrical conductivity. Nevertheless, the interface adhesion of CNTs-Cu is weak because of poor wettability and fragile reaction activity [12]. Generally, the surface chemical modification of CNTs (including decorated with metal or ceramic particles or coatings on CNT surface) could reduce the aggregation tendency and has also ameliorated the interfacial wettability via the formation of the transition region [13,14]. However, the introduction of transition phase would lead to the interfacial phonon and electron scattering, which will weaken thermal and electrical conductivities of composites. It is a pressing issue to achieve the homogeneous dispersion of CNTs and strong interface bonding for acquiring CNTs/Cu composites with excellent comprehensive properties.

Promisingly, carbonized polymer dots (CPD), one typical “star” material of the carbon nanomaterials (CNM) family, has the structural characteristics analogous with graphene. Furthermore, it possesses rich surface oxygen-containing functional groups, which are benefit for the electrons transfer and uniform dispersion in polar solvent [15–17]. CPD utilized as a reinforcement for Cu composites has brought new chance to solve the agglomeration and improve interface bonding via forming Cu-O-C covalent bond. In our previous report [18], CPD/Cu composites was prepared by the powder metallurgy and achieved higher increment of strength and conductivity as compared with CNTs/Cu composites. Therefore, it is a promising strategy to “grafting” CPD on CNTs surfaces (CPD@CNTs), which could improve the CNTs dispersibility by means of the abundant polar functional groups on CPD surface. In order to achieve this aim, undoubtedly, the tight adhesion of the two types of nanocarbons is vital guarantee [19]. Utilizing CPD@CNTs as a new formed reinforcement would overcome the inadequate intrinsic properties of the sole type and combine their respective merits [20,21].

Keeping the above considerations in mind, CPD@CNTs reinforcements were synthesized via in-situ synthesis process, which derived from the new synthesized CPD nanoparticles decorated CNTs surfaces. CPD@CNTs reinforced Cu composite was fabricated through the shift-speed ball milling and followed by SPS. The CPD@CNTs were homogeneously distributed within Cu matrix and enhanced the interface bonding. The effects of CPD@CNTs on the microstructure, mechanical properties and fracture features of composites were clarified, meanwhile, the role of CPD@CNTs on the electrical conductivity of composite was also investigated to account for the maintenance of functional property.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the hybrid CPD@CNTs powders
ce:section.ce:para: CNTs decorated with carbonized polymer dots (CPD@CNTs) was synthesized by a typical one-step hydrothermal method. Detailly, 0.5 g pristine multiwalled CNTs with diameter of 20–30 nm and length of <10 μm were offered by Chengdu Organic Chemistry Co. Ltd., China, dispersed in deionized water (100 mL) under ultrasonication for 30 min. 30 g citric acid (CA) and 6 mL ethylenediamine (EDA) were purchased from Shanghai Mclean Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd., China. They were dissolved in deionized water under magnetic stirring to obtain a homogeneous mixture solution. The CNTs suspension was then added to the mixture solution and stirred for 10 min. The mixed suspension was then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel and heated at 150 °C for 5 h. After the freeze-drying process, CPD@CNTs powders was obtained as expected and the weight ratio between CNTs and CPD is 3:1.

Electrolytic Cu powders (particle size 325 mesh, purity 99.9 %, obtained from Shanghai Naiou Nano Science and Technology Co. Ltd., China) was mixed with CPD@CNTs powders (ultrasonic dispersed in alcohol for 30 min) by shift-speed ball milled in ethanol medium, which includes low-speed ball milling (100 rpm for 8 h) and high-speed ball milling (200 rpm for 5 h), with a ball-to-powders weight ratio of 10:1. After filtration and vacuum drying, the prepared CPD@CNTs/Cu composite powders were annealed at 300 °C in an N2–10%H2 atmosphere for 5 h, followed by spark plasma sintering (SPS) at 750 °C for 5 min in a graphite mold with an inner diameter of 20 mm. A schematic illustration of the experimental procedure for the preparation of CPD@CNTs/Cu composite is shown in Fig. 1 . For comparison, pure Cu sample and Cu matrix composites reinforced with individual CNTs and CPD were also prepared under the identical milling and consolidation conditions, and all of composites have the same reinforcement contents of 0.2 wt%.

The new synthesized CPD and CPD@CNTs powders were analyzed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-F200, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) was utilized to analyze the surface chemistry of CPD. The microstructures of sintered samples were studied by optical microscope (OM, AXIO Scope.A1), and the average grain size was estimated via the line intercept method. Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, ZEISS Sigma 300) was employed to characterize the reinforcements distribution in bulk composite. The interfacial microstructures of composite were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, TecnaiG2-TF30, S-Twin). Electronic conductivity of the composites was measured using a portable electronic conductivity meter (Sigma 2008B), and hardness testing of the composites was conducted on Micro Vickers hardness tester under an applied load of 0.98 N and dwell time of 15 s. The mechanical properties were tested at room temperature using a universal tensiometer (AG-IS10KN) at a tensile rate of 0.2 mm/min. Dog-bone shaped tensile specimens with a gauge length of 16 mm, a gauge width of 1.5 mm and a thickness of 1.2 mm were used for the tensile tests at room temperature.

Morphologies of as-received CNTs and synthesized CPD and CPD@CNTs powders are presented in Fig. 2 . The morphologies of CPD are shown in Figs. 2(a-b), which can observe the near spherical microstructure and lattice fringe of CPD. Meanwhile, CPD has good ethanol solubility and displays fluorescence feature under the excitation of ultraviolet light (see inset in Fig. 2(a)) [22,23]. The original CNTs demonstrates regular walls and a hollow central channel with a width of ∼25 nm and length <10 μm (in Fig. 2(c-d)), it shows severe agglomeration (see Fig. 2(c)). The insert of Fig. 2(c) displays the pristine CNTs settle to the bottom of beaker, demonstrating inferior ethanol solubility of as-received CNTs. Fig. 2(d) shows that there are amorphous carbon nanolayer and defect on the pristine CNTs surface, these defective sites may provide the nucleation sites for CPD.

Figs. 2(e-f) present TEM images of mono-dispersed CPD@CNTs reinforcements, in-situ formed CPD leads to the roughness of CNTs surface, and the average size of CPD is ∼3.58 nm (in Fig. 2(g)). It is confirmed that strong interface adhesion between CPD and CNTs is formed as revealed by HRTEM image (see Fig. 2(f)). And it shows the intrinsic structure of CNTs is barely destroyed during the in-situ synthesis process of CPD. In order to further characterize CPD, Fourier transform image (FFT) and Inverse Fourier transform image (IFFT) marked by white frame in Fig. 2(f) were displayed in Figs. 2(I-II), the diffraction spots of (10
1 ¯
0), (01
1 ¯
0) and (
1 ¯
100) crystal planes of the CPD have marked (in Fig. 2(I)). And IFFT pattern in Fig. 2(II) shows interplanar spacing of (10
1 ¯
0), (01
1 ¯
0) and (
1 ¯
100) crystal planes of CPD are 0.21 nm, which correspond to the crystal planes of graphite [22]. Excitedly, after implanting the CPD on CNTs surface, CPD@CNTs has achieved uniform distribution in ethanol without obvious sedimentation (it has been verified by the insert of Fig. 2(e)), which can be ascribed to the excellent ethanol dispersion of CPD.

To further explore the crystal structures of various reinforcements and surface chemistry of CPD, XRD and XPS analyses were performed. Fig. 3(a) shows the XRD pattern of CPD, CNTs and CPD@CNTs powders. CPD can be seen that only one broad peak appears at about 2θ = 18°, which is caused by the highly disordered carbon atoms [17]. CNTs exist one high-intensity peak at 2θ = 26° (0002) and one low-intensity peak at 2θ = 43° (10
1 ¯
0) due to the graphene structure [7]. Interestingly, CPD@CNTs powders have combined the peaks characteristic of CPD and CNTs. Thus, from another aspect, it is confirmed that CPD has formed on CNTs surfaces. XPS survey spectra of CPD is indicated in Fig. 3(b), three primary peaks of this survey spectra are found at 284.8 eV, 399.7 eV and 531.1 eV, respectively, and these three peaks were characteristic for C1s, N1s and O1s. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s is displayed in Fig. 3(c), with three peaks corresponding to carbon atoms in different functional groups, i.e., the non‑oxygenated ring C (C=C/C-C, 284.8 eV), the carbonyl C (CO, 286.3 eV) and the carboxylate C (CO, 288.4 EV) [9]. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s is shown in Fig. 3(d), with three peaks for carbonyl group, hydroxyl, and carboxyl at 531.4 eV, 532.7 eV and 533.6 eV, respectively [23]. These functionalized groups with negatively charged and hydrophilic moieties render CPD good dispersibility. The result clarifies that the “grafting” of CPD on the CNTs surfaces could promote dispersion of CNTs via inducing abundant oxygen-functionalized groups. And these groups (CO and CO) can not only improve interface wettability of CNTs and Cu matrix but promote electron transport at interface [24].

Figs. 4 (a-h) show the optical micrographs and corresponding grain size distribution of pure Cu and Cu composites. No obvious pores or cracks appears, indicating that these sintered materials have high relative density. Pure Cu exhibits the coarsened and equiaxed grains with randomized orientations, and its average grain size is ∼6.68 μm (in Fig. 4(a, e)). By the incorporation of nano-fillers, the average grain sizes decrease to 6.65 μm, 2.92 μm and 2.35 μm for CPD/Cu, CNTs/Cu and CPD@CNTs/Cu composites, respectively. It indicates that CNTs could significantly refine the grains, CNTs pinning at grain boundaries can hinder the migration and growth of Cu grains, leading to the grain refinement, which is advantageous to the enhancement of mechanical properties [25]. The reinforcements distribution state in CNTs/Cu and CPD@CNTs/Cu bulk composites was further characterized by SEM. As shown in Fig. 4(i), CNTs entangle with each other and form CNTs clusters in CNTs/Cu composite, which would cause stress concentration during loading. Comparatively, CPD@CNTs is uniform dispersed in CPD@CNTs/Cu composite (in Fig. 4(j)) without agglomeration. Therefore, CNTs decorated with CPDs is benefit for the achievement of CPD@CNTs mono-dispersion in bulk matrix. The good dispersion is the precondition for achieving outstanding overall performances.

The interface structure between CPD@CNTs and Cu matrix is shown in Fig. 5 . Observed under low magnification TEM, CPD@CNTs reinforcements are observed alongside the Cu grain boundaries, as indicated in Fig. 5(a) with white arrow. It is noted that much dislocations (in Fig. 5(a)) distribute within Cu grains near the interface area. The distinct mismatch in coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) exist between CNTs and Cu matrix (CTE for CNT is 1.0 × 10−6 K−1 and CTE for Cu is 17.0 × 10−6 K−1 [7]) generated abundant dislocations during consolidation process. These dislocations are necessary to accommodate the pronounced internal stress caused through mismatched CTE [26]. Fig. 5(b) shows the bright-field TEM image with the corresponding energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), CPD@CNTs reinforcements homogeneously disperses in Cu matrix and has been affirmed by EDS result.

HRTEM image of CPD@CNTs interface morphology is observed in Fig. 5(c). Clean and seamless interfaces are observed and indicates the strong interface bonding between CPD and CNTs. It is worth noting that the in-situ formed Cu2O transition region was found near the interface of CPD@CNTs and Cu matrix. As shown in Fig. 5(d-e), the interfaces between Cu2O and Cu are also free of voids or gaps. IFFT images marked by white frame in Fig. 5(II) shows the (0002) planes of CNTs are screwy, indicating the prominent internal stress generated by the formation of Cu2O [9]. Due to there is abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on CPD@CNTs, Cu2O transition phase would form through the diffusion of O, Cu atoms. The appearance of Cu2O is also confirmed by HRTEM image (see Fig. 5(f)), in which observes the lattice planes of (111) Cu2O and (002) Cu2O (interplanar spacing are 0.24 and 0.21 nm, respectively). Meanwhile, the result demonstrates that CPD@CNTs and Cu2O phase was closely superimposed, which verified formation the tight interface bonding between CPD@CNTs and Cu2O.

Fig. 6(a) shows the histogram of relative density and electrical conductivity of pure Cu and composites. The relative density is roughly the same, and CPD@CNTs/Cu even reaches 98 %. The electrical conductivity (EC) of CNTs/Cu and CPD/Cu composites are 93.6%IACS and 95.9 %IACS (International Annealed Copper Standard), respectively. The main factors determine the resistivity of materials are defect scattering, phonon scattering and grain boundary or interface scattering [27]. For CNTs/Cu composite, the introduction of excessive interfaces would cause the probability of interface scattering. Comparatively, CPD has unique “core-shell” microstructure, and its center of the sphere is distributed with a few layers of graphene on the nano-plane scale, which is beneficial to electrons transfer [16]. Correspondingly, CPD/Cu has higher EC than CNTs/Cu. Meanwhile, CPD@CNTs/Cu composite achieves approximately equivalent EC (95.3 %IACS) as compared with CPD/Cu composite. It is ascribed that CPD covered on CNTs surface will form strong interface bonding between CPD@CNTs and Cu matrix, reducing the electron scattering effect and providing extra channels for electron transfer.

The mechanical properties of sintered samples were further measured by the quasi-static tensile tests. Fig. 6(b) exhibits the representative engineering stress-strain curves of pure Cu and Cu composites. The mechanical properties are also summarized in Table. 1 . The addition of individual CNTs does not achieve prospective strength enhancement, which can be attributed to the CNTs agglomeration within CNTs/Cu composite. The yield strength (YS) and tensile strength (UTS) of CPD/Cu composite are 164 MPa and 271 MPa, respectively, which are 53 % and 18 % higher than that of pure Cu (YS: 107 MPa, UTS: 229 MPa). It is note that CPD@CNTs/Cu composite exhibit the highest YS of 269 MPa and UTS of 308 MPa, demonstrating 152 % and 34 % increments to pure Cu, respectively. Meanwhile, compared with the composites with single CPD or CNTs reinforcement, the substantial improvement in tensile strength does not sacrifice the ductility in CPD@CNTs/Cu composite, still maintains a good elongation of 43 %. The hardness of composites displays similar trend with tensile properties (see Fig. 6(a)), CPD@CNTs/Cu composite has the highest value of 117 HV. Such results demonstrate that CNTs decorated with CPD nanoparticles (CPD@CNTs) is an effective strategy for the enhanced overall performances in Cu composites. The comparations of elongation and tensile strength of Cu composites reinforced with different carbon nanomaterials are shown in Fig. 6(c). The conventional Cu composite reinforced with individual CNTs, graphene or CNTs+graphene hybrid nano-fillers demonstrate a lower left trend compared with CPD@CNTs reinforcement. Therefore, CPD@CNTs/Cu composite in this work has achieved impressive balance between the elongation and tensile strength.

To illuminate the mechanism of fracture, the fracture morphologies of pure Cu and composites are shown in Fig. 7 . A number of deep dimples were observed on the fractured surface of pure Cu (Fig. 7(a)), which confirms the typical ductile fracture. The surfaces of composites also have much dimples (see Figs. 7(b-d)), the fracture characteristic of CPD/Cu composite is similar to pure Cu, with dense fined dimple structure and tear ridges were observed (as shown by arrows in Fig. 7(b)). The fractured surface of CNTs/Cu composite is shown in Fig. 7(c), CNTs clusters can be observed clearly, which will act as crack sources to worse the mechanical properties. Meanwhile, some fine dimples can be observed near CNTs clusters, indicating that CNTs/Cu composite happened the local plastic deformation. Fig. 7(d) shows the fracture feature of CPD@CNTs/Cu composite, nearly round dimples have been observed and some fractured CPD@CNTs reinforcements existed in the interior of the dimples. It demonstrates that the applied load has been transferred from matrix to CPD@CNTs reinforcements because of the tight interface bonding.

The CPD@CNTs/Cu composite exhibits good mechanical properties, significantly higher than that of the CNTs/Cu and CPD/Cu composites in this work. Herein, to further understand the strengthening behavior of the CPD@CNTs/Cu composite, the dominant strengthening mechanism is clarified: (i) grain refinement; (ii) thermal mismatch strengthening; (iii) the strengthening effects of CPD nanoparticles; (iv) the strengthening by CNTs. The yield strength contribution of the composite can be expressed as:
(1)
σ c
=
σ ym
+ Δ
σ GR
+ Δ
σ CTE
+ Δ
σ CPD
+ Δ
σ CNTs
where
σ c
and
σ ym
are the YS of composite and pure Cu matrix respectively and
Δσ GR
,
Δσ CTE
,
Δσ CPD
and
Δσ CNTs
are the strengthening contributions from grain refinement, thermal mismatch strengthening, the strengthening by CPD nanoparticles and the strengthening by CNTs, respectively.

According to the existing research, the CNTs/Cu composites generally exhibit grain refinement phenomenon during the sintering process. The average grain size measurement results through optical micrograph are shown in Fig. 4. It is no surprise that pure Cu has the largest average grain size (6.68 μm), and the CPD@CNTs/Cu composite with grain size (2.35 μm). The improvement of YS by grain refinement can be expressed as [27]:
(2)
Δ GR
= K
d c
− 0.5
−
d m
− 0.5
where K is a constant, which is 0.07 MPa.m0.5 for Cu,
d c
and
d m
are the average grain sizes for the composite and pure Cu, respectively. The value of
Δσ GR
for the CPD@CNTs/Cu composite is calculated to be 19 MPa.

Fig. 5(a) shows much dislocations distribute within Cu grains near the interface area, which will generate strengthening effects (
Δσ CTE
). This is because of the distinct mismatch in coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) between CNTs, CPD and Cu matrix (CTE for CPD and CNTs are 1.0 × 10−6 K−1 and CTE for Cu is 17.0 × 10−6 K−1 [7]). And the value can be assessment via the following equation [25].
(3)
Δ
σ CET
= 1.25 Gb
α
V f
Δ T Δ CTE
bd

where G
and b are the shear modulus (42.1 GPa) and burgers vector (0.256 nm) of Cu. α
is the geometric constant which is theoretically equal to 4 (for CNTs fibers [34]), d
is the mean equivalent diameter of reinforcements. ∆ T and ∆ CTE correspond to the difference in temperature and CTE respectively. In this calculation, CPD nanoparticles can be ignored because of the same CTE of CNTs and CPD, while CPD coated on CNTs surface.
V f
is the volume fraction of CNTs, 0.63 vol% in present work, converted from their corresponding weight fraction. For CNTs, the d =
3

l CNTs
are diameter (25 nm) and length (5 μm) of CNTs, respectively [9]. The contribution of
Δσ CTE
for the CPD@CNTs/Cu composite is calculated to be 42 MPa.

The strength effect of CPD nanoparticles on surface of CNTs attribute to CPD nanoparticles play a role in dislocation strengthening through the accumulation of dislocations around the nanoparticles. The strengthening contribution of
σ CPD
can be expressed as [35,36]:
(4)

is calculated to 27 MPa.

CNTs has the large aspect ratio and firmly interface bonding with Cu matrix, therefore the external load can be effectively transferred from Cu matrix to the reinforcement through the shear strength. According to the load transfer model in MMCs developed by Kelly and Tyson Eq. (8–10) [38]:
(8)

where

is the tensile strength of the CNT (Tensile strength of CNTs = 30 GPa [39]),

is the CNTs volume fraction (0.63 vol%, converted from their corresponding weight fraction),
l c
is the critical length of CNTs, l and d are the length (5 μm) and diameter (25 nm) of the CNTs which can be estimated from the experimental CNTs raw materials in Fig. 2.
σ my
is the YS of matrix and the value is taken as 107 MPa in previous study,
τ y
is the interfacial shear stress generated along the CNTs surface. According to Eq. (10), the calculated critical length
l c
is about 7 μm. The length l (5 μm) of CNTs is smaller than the calculated
l c
(7 μm) in this research. Therefore, the ∆
σ CNT
is calculated to 67 MPa through Eq. (9).

The experimental value and estimated tensile yield strength contribution of CPD@CNT/Cu composite is shown in Fig. 8 . It indicates that the calculated yield strength has a good agreement with the experimental values. And the strengthening by CNTs is the main part to enhance the strength of the composite.

According to the characteristic results, the schematic diagram of the CPD@CNTs distribution and corresponding fracture behaviors is shown in Fig. 9 . Distinguished with CNTs agglomeration in CNTs/Cu composite, CPD@CNTs are evenly distributed in Cu matrix, which can be ascribed to the nanocarbon structure design via CPD in-situ formed on the CNTs surfaces. And the fracture of CPD@CNTs, which demonstrates that the interface bonding between CPD@CNTs and Cu matrix is strong. It shows that CPD at the interface can not only hinder CNTs pulled out from Cu matrix but accumulate dislocations, thus enhance the tensile strength of CPD@CNTs/Cu composite. Meanwhile, Cu2O transition phase has improved the wettability of carbon nanomaterials and Cu matrix and facilitates the load transfer from Cu to CPD@CNTs.

In summary, CNTs decorated with CPD was synthesized through a typical one-step hydrothermal method. Due to CPD has abundant oxygen-containing functional groups with negatively charged and hydrophilic moieties, it would in turn promote the dispersion of CNTs. Meanwhile, it would improve the interfacial bonding between CNTs and Cu matrix via in-situ formed Cu2O transition phase. Tensile tests indicated that CPD@CNTs/Cu composite exhibited the highest YS (269 MPa) and UTS (308 MPa), which was 147 % and 38 % higher than those of CNTs/Cu composite. Moreover, the electrical conductivity of CPD@CNTs/Cu composite was 95.3 %IACS, which was ascribed that CPD covered on CNTs would reduce the electron scattering effect via forming strong interface bonding and provided extra channels for electron transfer. The structure regulation of CNTs decorated with CPD is helpful for achieving balances of strength-ductility-conductivity, it has offered a new strategy of reinforcement structure design for improving comprehensive properties in Cu matrix composites.
Wenfu Zhang: Investigation, Writing- original draft;
Liang Liu: Project administration, Conceptualization;
Rui Bao: Project administration, Methodology;
Jianhong Yi: Writing - review & editing;
Yajie Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology;
Caiju Li: Analysis;
Xiaofeng Chen: Validation;
Shengda Guo: Review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
